interview_metadata.interviewee_title: St. Augustine Civil Rights Commemoration Organizer; Co-founder, 40th ACCORD
interview_metadata.interviewer_affiliation: Southern Oral History Program, for the Library of Congress and Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture

main_summary.summary:
Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan, a fifth-generation native of Lincolnville in St. Augustine, Florida (born June 28, 1956), recounts her family's deep roots in the area and her childhood memories of the civil rights movement that came to her hometown when she was seven and a half. She traces her ancestry through her great-great grandfather Thomas A. Finley, who migrated from the Carolinas through Georgia and eventually settled with thirteen children in Moultrie, just south of St. Augustine in St. Johns County. Her great-grandmother Abby Finley was a founder of St. Cyprian's Episcopal Church in Lincolnville, which Duncan says has stood for over 150 years.

Duncan describes growing up in segregated Lincolnville as initially insulated and self-contained, with Black-owned grocery stores, beauty parlors, bars on Washington Street, newspaper publishers, and the church as the center of social life. Her awareness of segregation came when she ventured downtown around age ten or twelve, where she encountered "colored" signs at the doctor's building on Treasury Street and was funneled to a back entrance. She recounts childhood memories of Ku Klux Klan night marches through Lincolnville, her mother holding her hand and warning her not to look back, classmates' homes being firebombed near Excelsior Elementary School, and a fragmented memory of Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. crossing the street from Mr. Proctor's house to ask her mother about attending a rally. She describes the violence and rejection she encountered when forced school integration moved her out of Excelsior into previously white schools beginning in sixth grade, where teachers were reluctant to teach Black students and white students formed lines to block them from reaching lunch.

The second half of the interview centers on Duncan's adult work with the 40th ACCORD, the organization she helped found to commemorate the 40th anniversary of the St. Augustine civil rights movement. After her friend Mrs. Catherine Twine died in 2002, Duncan met with local historian David Nolan, J. T. Johnson of Atlanta, professor Jane Billings of Brown, Mrs. Cora Tyson Johnson, Dwight Hines, and Sandra Parks at the South Seas restaurant to plan the commemoration; she proposed framing it as a family reunion inviting movement participants back to St. Augustine. Northrop Grumman funded thirty historic civil rights markers placed in and around the city, many on private citizens' yards. Duncan honors Dr. Robert B. Hayling, the local foot soldiers including Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson, and Mrs. Reena Ayers, who at nearly 107 had given lodging and food to demonstrators and Northern attorneys. The 40th ACCORD is now working toward a civil rights museum in St. Augustine to coincide with the 50th anniversary of the Civil Rights Act in 2014, with Richard Burton of Jacksonville as chair and J. T. Johnson as co-chair.

Throughout, Duncan expresses both pride in the sacrifices made by movement participants and grief that St. Augustine has not changed as much as those sacrifices warranted: Black businesses are few, Black representation in city and county government is minimal, and her own son, who holds engineering and construction degrees from the University of Florida, could not find work in his hometown and has settled in Jacksonville. She quotes Cora Tyson Johnson's image of a "hidden vapor" still hanging over the town and Hank Thomas's characterization of St. Augustine as "paternalistic," and she connects her family's centuries-long presence to the region's history as a Black sanctuary, citing the first free Black settlement at Fort Mose as a possible reason her ancestors traveled so far.
key_themes: Multigenerational Black family history in Lincolnville, St. Augustine
Childhood witness of Ku Klux Klan terror and Dr. King's St. Augustine campaign
Forced school integration and the violence of being unwanted
Local civil rights commemoration and the 40th ACCORD
Recovering hidden Black history and naming local foot soldiers
Persistent economic and political exclusion of Black St. Augustine
Generational continuity: from Fort Mose sanctuary to twenty-first-century departure
main_summary.historical_significance: Duncan's testimony is one of the few first-person childhood accounts of the 1963-64 St. Augustine campaign from a Lincolnville resident who later became an organizer of the campaign's institutional memory. It documents the founding of the 40th ACCORD and the placement of the St. Augustine civil rights markers, identifies surviving local participants by name (Dr. Robert B. Hayling, Audrey Nell Hamilton, JoeAnn Anderson, Mrs. Reena Ayers, Reverend Goldie Eubanks), and connects the 1964 movement to ongoing questions of economic justice in the city. It is also one of the personal-witness sources for how a child experienced the Klan night-marches and the integration of St. Augustine schools.

errors: Treasury Street identification is inferred — the SRT renders the street as 'trimmer's in street' (likely 'Treasury Street' but the transcript spelling is garbled and the canonical name is not directly stated)

Mrs. Reena Ayers's age is paraphrased as 'nearly 107'; the speaker says 'she'll be a hundred and seven years old this year,' which is closer to 'about to turn 107' — the summary's wording is acceptable but slightly less precise

The summary attributes 'Excelsior Elementary School' to the school Duncan attended, which the transcript supports (segment 72), but the school she transferred to in sixth grade is not named in the transcript and the summary correctly does not name it

Jane Billings is described as 'professor of Brown' in the transcript but Duncan herself qualifies: 'she was a professor at one of the schools. I think it was Spellman in Atlanta.' The summary describes her as 'professor Jane Billings of Brown' which carries Duncan's ambiguity through but slightly underplays Duncan's own uncertainty about the institution
improvements: Soften the Treasury Street identification with 'a downtown doctor's building' since the SRT spelling is garbled
Rephrase Reena Ayers's age as 'about to turn 107' to match the speaker's exact framing
Note Duncan's own uncertainty about Jane Billings's institutional affiliation (the speaker says 'I think it was Spellman in Atlanta')
Add explicit qualifier that the Fort Mose connection is Duncan's own inference about her ancestors' motivation, not an established genealogical claim

chapters.title: Interview setup and introduction in St. Augustine
chapters.summary: Joe Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program introduces the interview as a joint undertaking of the Library of Congress and the Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture, conducted with videographer John Bishop at the Bayfront Hilton in St. Augustine, Florida on Tuesday, September 13, 2011. Mosnier identifies the interviewee as Mrs. Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan and notes that the conversation will cover local civil rights history and her ongoing involvements.
related_events: Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture

improvements: Brief chapter; no significant gaps. Could mention the project's joint Library of Congress / Smithsonian framing more prominently.

chapters.title: Lincolnville roots and the Finley family genealogy
chapters.summary: Duncan introduces herself as a fifth-generation native of Lincolnville, born June 28, 1956 in St. Augustine, Florida. She traces her ancestry through her great-great grandfather Thomas A. Finley, who moved from the Carolinas. He and his thirteen children traveled from South Carolina through Georgia, eventually settling in Moultrie, just south of St. Augustine in St. Johns County, on about thirteen acres as a farmer. His daughter, Duncan's great-grandmother Abby Finley, married a man also named Abby Finley and bought land in Lincolnville. Duncan notes that the St. Augustine Record, which 'did not mention black people often,' reported on his death at age eighty-nine, observing only that 'he served the white community well.' He brought wares from Moultrie to St. Augustine before moving into Lincolnville.

errors: The transcript at segment 24 calls the daughter 'Abby Finley' and says 'She married her husband, Abby Finley' — this appears to be a Whisper transcription error and may obscure whether the husband and the daughter shared the name; the summary carries the transcript's wording through without claiming clarity
improvements: Flag the Abby/Abby naming ambiguity as a transcript artifact rather than imputing a clean genealogy

chapters.title: St. Cyprian's Church and growing up in segregated Lincolnville
chapters.summary: Duncan describes her great-grandmother Abby Finley as a founder of St. Cyprian's Episcopal Church in Lincolnville, which she says has stood for over 150 years and still has a congregation. Her mother's grandmother Elizabeth lived directly across from the church. Duncan reflects on growing up in Lincolnville as fun and largely insulated from awareness of segregation: because of segregation, Black-owned grocery stores, beauty parlors, bars on Washington Street, and newspaper publishers were all present in the neighborhood, and the church served as the center of social activity with socials and picnics. She mentions being one of eight girls (no boys) raised in part by a stepfather she describes as a hard-working man. Her great-grandmother died at about eighty-nine or ninety; her grandmother on her mother's side died in November 1963, the same month President Kennedy was shot, an event Duncan can remember.

improvements: Could quote 'everything centered around the church' verbatim
The transcript's account of the grandmother named 'Elizabeth' is briefly noted but Duncan immediately corrects to 'Abby' — the chapter should reflect that hesitation more directly

chapters.title: Childhood memories of the Klan in Lincolnville
chapters.summary: Duncan recalls being seven and a half years old when Dr. King came to St. Augustine and her clearest memories from that period: marches by the Ku Klux Klan through Lincolnville. From a child's perspective they looked like 'some big parade' she wanted to participate in. At night the terror became palpable: she remembers Black residents lining the streets as the Klan marched through, her family going down to the corner of New Haven (her wording) and Central Avenue, now Martin Luther King Avenue, to watch. Walking home, her mother held her and her sister's hands and warned, 'don't look back.' Some of her classmates' homes on adjacent streets were firebombed.

errors: The transcript renders the corner as 'New Haven' (segment 42) — this is likely a Whisper rendering of a different street name (possibly 'Bridge' or 'Lovett' or similar local St. Augustine cross-street); the summary carries it through as Duncan said but with quotation framing
improvements: Could quote the 'don't look back' moment verbatim
Note that the firebombings claim is general ('some of my classmates') without specific dates

chapters.title: Badcock Furniture, the 'bad cops,' and Dr. King's morning visit
chapters.summary: Duncan describes a confused childhood memory of 'Badcock Furniture' — a store name she conflated with 'bad cops' and the distrust of police felt by Black residents of Lincolnville. She then recounts a faded memory of one morning when Dr. King came across the street from where he was staying at Mr. Proctor's house to ask Duncan's mother on the porch whether she would participate in a rally or marches that night. Years later Duncan asked her mother whether this actually happened, and her mother confirmed it did.
related_events: Mr. Proctor (host of MLK in St. Augustine)

errors: The summary identifies 'Mr. Proctor's house' as Dr. King's lodging based on Duncan's statement at segment 47; Duncan herself says 'I don't know if he stayed there that night,' so the summary should not imply that Mr. Proctor was Dr. King's host with certainty
improvements: Soften the Mr. Proctor lodging claim to 'a house across the street identified as Mr. Proctor's'
Note Duncan's own framing of the memory as 'faded' until her mother confirmed it

chapters.title: Encountering segregation at the downtown doctor's office
chapters.summary: Around age ten to twelve, Duncan began venturing downtown and experienced segregation directly. She recalls a 'colored' sign on the wall of the doctor's building near Flagler Hospital, on a downtown street the transcript renders as 'trimmer's in street.' Black patients were directed right and around to a back entrance, with a forbidden straight hallway leading from the front. She remembers when the sign came down — even after the wall was painted, the spot was still visible. She took her younger sister to the doctor and, finding the sign gone, walked down the previously forbidden front hallway. She and her sister sat in the front waiting area and were called first, but were then walked back through the office to the rear waiting room. The interviewer notes this would have been 1966–1968, after the Civil Rights Act of 1964, and Duncan agrees she cannot remember the exact year.

errors: The street name is rendered 'trimmer's in street' in the SRT — the summary preserves the garbled rendering rather than guessing 'Treasury Street' or another canonical name
improvements: Could quote Duncan's 'communicable disease' line verbatim — she says 'everybody looked at you as though you had a communicable disease'
Note that the interviewer's date-range (1966-1968) is the interviewer's inference, not Duncan's testimony

chapters.title: Forced school integration and the fight to be educated
chapters.summary: Duncan describes completing fifth grade at Excelsior Elementary School in St. Augustine. In sixth grade her school was closed by forced integration and she was sent to a previously white school. She recalls that the new teachers did not want to teach Black students and that white children would not sit beside her. She believed she would have been valedictorian or salutatorian had her class remained at Excelsior. The junior high school had no cafeteria, so students had to walk back to Orange Street to eat lunch, where white students would form a line to block Black students from passing. Duncan emphasizes that 'it wasn't non-violence' in the schools — that students had to fight 'just to eat lunch.' Duncan and her cohort eventually graduated from St. Augustine High School, but she says the atmosphere of not belonging persisted, and her own children later experienced it as well.

improvements: Could quote 'it wasn't non-violence. We had to fight even to eat' verbatim
The specific new sixth-grade school is not named in the transcript and the summary correctly avoids naming it

chapters.title: Founding the 40th ACCORD and the South Seas meeting
chapters.summary: Duncan describes how the 40th ACCORD organization began. She credits the Twines (including her late friend Mrs. Catherine Twine, who died in 2002) and Dr. Robert B. Hayling, whom she learned about later in life. After Catherine Twine's funeral, a group met at the South Seas restaurant on the island to discuss commemorating the upcoming 40th anniversary of the St. Augustine civil rights movement: local historian David Nolan, J. T. Johnson from Atlanta, Jane Billings of Brown (whom Duncan says was a professor at one of the schools, possibly Spellman in Atlanta), Mrs. Cora Tyson Johnson, Dwight Hines (Duncan was uncertain of his last name), and Sandra Parks. David Nolan asked Duncan to come up with ideas; she proposed framing the commemoration as a family reunion, inviting movement participants back to St. Augustine, modeled on her own family reunion in 2001 in which her daughter's genealogy research had uncovered a cousin who served as ambassador to Madagascar under President Clinton.

errors: Jane Billings's institutional affiliation is uncertain — Duncan says 'I think it was Spellman in Atlanta' but the standard spelling is 'Spelman'; the summary preserves Duncan's hesitation
The transcript says David Nolan emailed her after the meeting; the summary glosses this slightly as 'asked Duncan to come up with ideas'
improvements: Quote Duncan's 'put on my thinking cap' verbatim
Note that the Madagascar ambassador cousin was found through Duncan's daughter's genealogy research, not Duncan's own

chapters.title: Hidden history and the cost of remembering
chapters.summary: Duncan describes the Gallimore Center strategic meeting that followed, where older participants began sharing 'incredible stories' and 'heart wrenching stories' of the terror they had lived through during the St. Augustine movement led by Dr. Robert B. Hayling and Dr. King. At one meeting at the De Soto place, J. T. Johnson flew in from Atlanta; a member of the audience asked, 'why don't you all let sleeping dogs lie?' J. T. Johnson stood and said, 'it's just time to just say thank you.' Duncan emphasizes that the movement's history had been hidden by St. Augustine's power structures, which had not wanted to acknowledge what had happened. She invokes Fort Mose — which she calls the first free Black settlement in the United States — as a possible explanation for why her ancestors came so far to St. Augustine, and she expresses grief that despite the sacrifices of Dr. Robert Hayling and Dr. Martin Luther King, some things remain unchanged.

errors: Fort Mose is Duncan's claim — she names it as 'the first free black settlement in the United States.' This is historically accurate (Fort Mose, established 1738 near St. Augustine, is generally recognized as the first legally sanctioned free Black community in what is now the United States), but the summary should signal it is Duncan's framing, not an objective claim
The 'Soto place' street name is preserved from the transcript spelling; the canonical reference is likely the historic De Soto location in St. Augustine
improvements: Add explicit qualifier that the Fort Mose connection is Duncan's hypothesis about her ancestors' motivation

chapters.title: Education for her children and Northrop Grumman's markers
chapters.summary: Duncan describes herself as the mother of five children to whom she has stressed education. She names a son with an industrial engineering and systems engineering degree from the University of Florida who returned to St. Augustine after graduation and could not find work — he was even qualified to teach geometry and trigonometry, but no employer in the city would hire him. He returned to school for a bachelor's degree in building and construction and still could not find work locally. He has written publicly that 'the city that he loved will never be the city that he will come back to ever again.' Duncan then describes Northrop Grumman's funding of thirty historic civil rights markers in and around St. Augustine, including markers placed on private citizens' yards; she notes the contrast with Fairchild (the predecessor company at that site), which she says was not friendly to civil rights demonstrators or to Black employees. Duncan names some of the figures the markers honor: Dr. Robert Hayling, the St. Augustine Foot Soldiers, Audrey Nell Hamilton, JoeAnn Anderson, and Mrs. Reena Ayers (who at almost 107 had given lodging and food to demonstrators and to Northern attorneys defending them in court).
related_events: Northrop Grumman civil rights marker funding

errors: The transcript renders the predecessor company name as 'Fertile Hill' (segment 218); the summary uses 'Fairchild' as the likely canonical name, but this is an inference from the audio context (Northrop Grumman acquired a Fairchild facility in St. Augustine), not a transcript-explicit identification
The son's first name is rendered 'Zebra Lendonkin' in segment 149 — likely a Whisper mangle; the summary avoids quoting the name and refers to him as 'a son'

The summary describes Mrs. Reena Ayers as having given lodging 'to Northern attorneys defending them in court' — the transcript phrasing is 'attorneys who came from the North to defend them in the courts'; the meaning is preserved
improvements: Either preserve the SRT's 'Fertile Hill' spelling with a note, or omit the predecessor company name entirely since it is a Whisper artifact
Could quote the son's public statement verbatim: 'the city that he loved will never be the city that he will come back to ever again'

chapters.title: Hopes for change and reactions to the Freedom Trail markers
chapters.summary: Duncan reflects on what has and has not changed in St. Augustine. She notes that there are few Black businesses (probably not enough to count on one hand), few Black officials in St. Augustine or St. Johns County government, and that young people have left and may not return. She mentions her son in Jacksonville (married to an internal-medicine physician) and a daughter with a doctorate in physical therapy considering moving away from St. Augustine. She invokes Cora Tyson Johnson's image of a 'hidden vapor' hanging over the town and Hank Thomas's description of St. Augustine as 'paternalistic.' She describes the reaction to the Freedom Trail markers as initially resistant but now improving, especially since Ambassador Andrew Young's previewing of his civil-rights film 'Crossing in St. Augustine' brought national exposure. Duncan acknowledges that the city's growing willingness to embrace civil rights history is partly motivated by tourism revenue.
related_events: Crossing in St. Augustine (Andrew Young film)

errors: The film title is rendered 'Crossing in St. Augustine' in segment 192; the canonical title is 'Crossing in St. Augustine' (Andrew Young's 2010 documentary) — this matches
Hank Thomas is named directly by Duncan at segment 228; the summary attributes the 'paternalistic' framing correctly
improvements: Could quote 'we need caring for, you know, as so we can't take care of ourselves' from Hank Thomas verbatim
Note that Duncan attributes the city's changing attitudes 'to me, in St. Johns County' specifically

chapters.title: What hasn't changed: jobs, exodus, and a vision for St. Augustine
chapters.summary: Duncan speaks more pointedly about what remains broken. There are not enough jobs or opportunities for Black St. Augustinians; the absence of industry has driven young people out. She invokes Ambassador Young's image of the markers as 'weeds growing up' — meaning they have proliferated despite institutional resistance. She wants to see St. Augustine retain its historic character at the city level while the county opens to industry so that Black families can stay and raise their children. She says that there used to be 'thousands of black people here' who have 'taken flight.' She also reflects on Northrop Grumman's funding as 'God's blessings,' contrasting it with the predecessor company's documented hostility to civil rights demonstrators and to Black employees.

errors: The 'weeds growing up' image is attributed to 'the ambassador, young' in the transcript; the summary attributes it to Ambassador Young correctly
improvements: Could note that Duncan says her daughter is 'thinking about moving to her wife for a little while' — this is almost certainly a Whisper artifact (likely 'moving up north' or 'moving for a while'); the summary correctly avoids quoting this

chapters.title: The 40th ACCORD's next steps and the civil rights museum
chapters.summary: Duncan outlines the 40th ACCORD's current and upcoming work. They commemorate the anniversary of the signing of the Civil Rights Act each year; in the most recent commemoration Dr. Dorothy Cotton was the keynote speaker, with Sonja Plummer (granddaughter of civil rights activist Reverend Goldie Eubanks) as a featured speaker. For July 2012 they are bringing Taylor Branch. They also lead Freedom Trail tours and have run two teacher workshops in St. Johns County with the superintendent's permission, including one at St. Augustine High School featuring Dr. Robert Hayling and Mrs. Lillian Twine Robeson; they produced a civil rights workbook with pictures and documents funded by Northrop Grumman. Duncan describes the 40th ACCORD's long-term goal as establishing a civil rights museum in St. Augustine. A committee — separate from the 40th ACCORD itself but spun off from it — is chaired by Richard Burton of Jacksonville, a national NAACP board member, with J. T. Johnson as co-chair. The president of Flagler Hospital and the mayor are also on the committee. The committee is aiming for 2014, in time for the 50th anniversary of the Civil Rights Act, with a more modest facility than the multimillion-dollar museum Ambassador Andrew Young hopes to fundraise.

errors: The transcript says the recent keynote was 'Dr. Dorothy Cotton' and a 'guess speaker was Sonja Plummer' — likely 'guest speaker'; the summary uses 'featured speaker' to convey the meaning
Sonja Plummer is described as 'granddaughter of one of the civil rights activists here, Reverend Goldie Eubanks' — the summary preserves this attribution
improvements: Could note that the museum committee 'come up from under the umbrella of accord because it's such a big adventure' — this is Duncan's framing

chapters.title: Closing thanks
chapters.summary: Mosnier thanks Duncan for her time and her work, expresses his anticipation of the museum and other plans moving forward, and closes the interview. He identifies the recording as a presentation of the Library of Congress and the Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture.
related_events: Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture

interview_metadata.interviewee_title: St. Augustine Civil Rights Commemoration Organizer; Co-founder, 40th ACCORD
interview_metadata.interviewer_affiliation: Southern Oral History Program, for the Library of Congress and Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture

evidence: Opens with family genealogy back to Thomas A. Finley and moves chronologically through childhood, segregation downtown, school integration, and adult activism (Segment 18, 00:02:02,000)
Transition from childhood memory of Klan marches to encountering Dr. King at the porch is handled organically (Segment 47, 00:07:26,000)
Closes with future-facing 40th ACCORD plans, returning the arc to civic action and the planned museum (Segment 250, 00:32:21,000)
story_structure_coherence.justification: The interview follows a clear chronological-then-thematic arc: ancestry, childhood under segregation and Klan terror, forced school integration, and adult commemorative work. Mosnier's prompts at key transitions help maintain structure. There are some small backtracking moments around the doctor's office sign and a few digressions on family detail, but the overall organization is strong.

most_vivid_moment.quote: And I can remember, as we were walking home, my mom holding my sister's hands in mine, and she said, don't look back.

evidence: Klan night-march memory with mother's grip and the warning 'don't look back' (Segment 43, 00:06:47,000)
The colored sign at the downtown doctor's office and the painted-over spot still visible (Segment 58, 00:09:13,000)
Walking through the forbidden front hallway with her younger sister after the sign came down (Segment 61-63, 00:09:46,000)
vivid_details_examples.justification: Duncan provides several very vivid, sensory childhood scenes — the Klan march, the doctor's office, the porch visit from Dr. King — and many named individuals (Thomas A. Finley, Abby Finley, Catherine Twine, Cora Tyson Johnson, J. T. Johnson, David Nolan, Sandra Parks, Dwight Hines, Dorothy Cotton, Taylor Branch, Lillian Twine Robeson, Reena Ayers, Audrey Nell Hamilton, JoeAnn Anderson, Goldie Eubanks, Richard Burton, Hank Thomas, Andrew Young, Robert B. Hayling, Martin Luther King Jr., John F. Kennedy, Sonja Plummer). Dates are mostly relative ('seven and a half years old,' 'November 1963') but a few are precise (June 28, 1956).

most_authentic_moment.quote: Things have changed. They're not the same even now and it makes me want to cry. It brings people tears to know that all that Dr. Robert Hayling, all that Dr. Martin Luther King did here in this town, there's something still remain the same.

vulnerability_elements: Visibly tearing up while speaking about unchanged conditions in St. Augustine ('I will wipe my tears away,' Segment 146)
Disclosing her son's inability to find work in his hometown despite multiple degrees (Segments 149-153)
Admitting that fragmented childhood memories required confirmation from her mother (Segment 49)
emotional_authenticity.justification: Duncan moves visibly between gratitude and grief, especially in the central passage about Dr. Hayling and Dr. King's sacrifices. She tears up on camera, acknowledges family disappointment with her son's departure, and speaks plainly about the city's failure. Her testimony is not performed; the emotional registers are integrated with the factual recounting.

narrative_quality.overall_assessment: Duncan's narrative quality is strong throughout. The structure is chronological-then-thematic with clear transitions. The vivid moments — especially the Klan march and the doctor's office — anchor the interview in sensory memory. Her emotional range moves from childhood fear to mature grief and gratitude, with on-camera tears at the central reflection on how little has changed. The narrative occasionally meanders (especially around family detail) and a few names are tentative ('Dwight Hines, I believe it was'), but overall the storytelling is honest and engaging.

major_events: 1963-64 St. Augustine civil rights campaign — witnessed at age 7-8 as a Lincolnville resident (Segment 37, 00:05:49,000)
Ku Klux Klan night marches through Lincolnville (Segment 41, 00:06:28,000)
Forced school integration in St. Augustine, sixth grade onward (Segment 73, 00:11:04,000)
Founding of the 40th ACCORD commemorative organization after 2002 (Segment 100, 00:13:53,000)
Placement of 30 Northrop Grumman-funded civil rights markers (Segment 163, 00:21:14,000)
Ambassador Andrew Young's 'Crossing in St. Augustine' film premiere (Segment 192, 00:25:33,000)

key_figures.name: Dr. Robert B. Hayling
key_figures.relationship: Honored elder; learned about him later; central figure of 40th ACCORD's commemorative work

key_figures.name: Mrs. Catherine Twine
key_figures.relationship: Friend; her 2002 death catalyzed the 40th ACCORD

key_figures.name: Mrs. Cora Tyson Johnson
key_figures.relationship: Co-organizer of the Gallimore Center strategic meeting

key_figures.name: J. T. Johnson
key_figures.relationship: Atlanta-based foot soldier who returned to St. Augustine for the commemorative meetings; co-chair of the museum committee

key_figures.name: David Nolan
key_figures.relationship: Local historian; co-conceiver of the 40th ACCORD

key_figures.name: Martin Luther King Jr.
key_figures.relationship: Crossed the street from Mr. Proctor's house to ask her mother about a rally

organizational_involvement.organization: St. Augustine civil rights museum committee
organizational_involvement.role: Founding committee member (under chair Richard Burton, co-chair J. T. Johnson)

unique_firsthand_perspective.unique_access: Duncan offers both a Lincolnville child's eyewitness perspective on the 1963-64 St. Augustine campaign and an organizational-history account of how the 40th ACCORD commemorative apparatus was built two decades after the fact.
unique_firsthand_perspective.justification: Duncan was a child witness to the campaign rather than a participant; her firsthand access is to the local Black community's experience of the violence and to the post-2002 commemorative effort. She is not a national-tier figure but is the principal organizing memory-keeper of the St. Augustine commemoration, which gives her unique access to that organizational history.

precision_examples: Great-grandfather Thomas A. Finley moved from Carolinas through Georgia to Moultrie with 13 children and 13 acres (Segments 19-22, 00:02:28,000)
Great-grandmother died at 89 or 90; grandmother died in November 1963 (Segments 35-36, 00:05:30,000)
Thirty Northrop Grumman-funded civil rights markers in and around the city (Segment 163, 00:21:14,000)
Mrs. Reena Ayers will be 107 years old this year (Segment 166, 00:22:10,000)
verifiable_claims: Catherine Twine died in 2002 (her funeral catalyzed the 40th ACCORD)
Northrop Grumman funded 30 historic civil rights markers in St. Augustine
Ambassador Andrew Young's film 'Crossing in St. Augustine' was previewed in St. Augustine
Dorothy Cotton was keynote speaker at the 40th ACCORD's commemoration of the Civil Rights Act signing in July (recent)
Taylor Branch was scheduled as a 40th ACCORD speaker for July 2012
St. Cyprian's Episcopal Church in Lincolnville is described as 'maybe over 150 years' old
specificity_of_details.justification: Specific full names, precise birth date, and verifiable organizational details are common; many dates remain relative ('seven and a half,' 'about 10, 11, 12,' 'two or three years ago'). The narrative quality is good but not exceptional in date precision.

new_information: Lived experience of Lincolnville under Klan terror from a child's perspective ('don't look back')
Organizational history of the 40th ACCORD and the South Seas restaurant founding meeting
Northrop Grumman's role funding 30 civil rights markers, contrasted with predecessor company's hostility
Local figures (Mrs. Reena Ayers, Reverend Goldie Eubanks, Sonja Plummer) who do not appear prominently in national civil rights historiography
Hank Thomas's 'paternalistic' framing of St. Augustine and Cora Tyson Johnson's 'hidden vapor' image
understudied_aspects: Childhood experience of segregation and Klan terror in Lincolnville
Forced school integration in St. Augustine's pre-existing all-white schools
Post-movement commemorative infrastructure-building in a town that resisted acknowledging its civil rights history
Economic exclusion of college-educated Black professionals in St. Augustine into the 2010s
fills_historical_gaps.narrative_complications: Duncan complicates the triumphalist narrative of post-Civil Rights Act progress by documenting that St. Augustine in 2011 still drives Black professionals away and is only now (and partly for tourism revenue) acknowledging its own history.
fills_historical_gaps.historiographical_value: Historians of the St. Augustine campaign and of post-movement commemorative practice can use Duncan's testimony to understand how local memory work was organized, who participated, and which figures the local Black community considers foundational beyond the well-known Hayling and King narratives.
fills_historical_gaps.justification: Duncan's testimony is a strong gap-filler for the local Lincolnville childhood perspective and for the organizational history of 40th ACCORD. The latter is genuinely understudied — most St. Augustine campaign scholarship focuses on 1963-64 and on Hayling/King, not on the post-2002 commemorative apparatus that Duncan helped build.

historical_value.overall_assessment: Duncan provides solid historical value. She is a child witness rather than a movement participant, but her access to the 40th ACCORD founding circle and the marker placement story gives her unique testimony about post-movement memory work in a resistant town. Her details on individual local figures (Reena Ayers, Goldie Eubanks, Sonja Plummer, Catherine Twine, Cora Tyson Johnson) are valuable additions to the St. Augustine sub-corpus.

transformation_growth.transformation_arc: From a Lincolnville child for whom Klan marches looked like 'some big parade' to a forced-integration teenager fighting to eat lunch, to an adult organizer of the 40th ACCORD commemoration who names herself a memory-keeper for the local civil rights movement's foot soldiers.
key_turning_points: Realization at the doctor's office that the colored sign coming down did not mean Black patients were welcome through the front (Segments 66-67, 00:10:21,000)
Forced integration into a previously white school and discovering 'it wasn't non-violence' inside (Segment 80, 00:11:50,000)
Catherine Twine's 2002 funeral and the South Seas restaurant meeting that launched the 40th ACCORD (Segments 100-108, 00:13:53,000)
transformation_growth.before_after_contrast: As a child Duncan did not know what segregation was; as an adult she organizes the commemorative infrastructure of St. Augustine's civil rights history and confronts the city's continuing exclusions of Black professionals (including her own son).
transformation_growth.inspirational_quality: Duncan's arc — from witness to memory-keeper — models how a private individual without national platform can build the institutional memory of a movement.
transformation_growth.justification: There is a real transformation arc here from child witness to adult organizer. Duncan reflects explicitly on what experiences shaped her, and the arc is honest rather than triumphalist. It is not as deeply explored as a leadership-track narrative would be (Duncan does not center her own development as much as the movement's), so the score is solid but not exceptional.

peak_moments.description: Klan night march through Lincolnville; mother holding the children's hands and saying 'don't look back'
peak_moments.stakes: Physical safety of the family in a town under racial terror

peak_moments.description: First sixth-grade day at the integrated school; teachers refusing to teach, white children blocking the lunch line
peak_moments.stakes: Right to education and basic dignity inside the newly desegregated schools

peak_moments.description: Public meeting confronted by an audience member asking 'why don't you all let sleeping dogs lie?' — J. T. Johnson stands and says 'it's just time to just say thank you'
peak_moments.stakes: Whether the local Black community can publicly commemorate the civil rights movement in St. Augustine

conflict_types: Racial terror (Klan night marches and firebombings)
Institutional resistance to remembering local civil rights history
Economic exclusion of Black professionals from the city's job market
tension_conflict_triumph.emotional_range: From childhood fear to adult grief to defiant gratitude
tension_conflict_triumph.justification: Duncan recounts several genuinely high-stakes moments — the Klan marches, the integrated school violence, the public confrontation about commemoration — with concrete stakes. The dramatic content is solid but is mostly recounted as memory rather than dramatically reconstructed in scene.

universal_themes: Sibling responsibility (taking her younger sister to the doctor)
Adult grief that earlier sacrifices have not fully paid off
Children leaving home for opportunities elsewhere
relatable_struggles: Confronting hate as a child too young to understand it
Watching a hometown fail to evolve
A child being unable to find work in his hometown despite credentials
human_moments: Mother saying 'don't look back' as Klan marches through Lincolnville (Segment 43, 00:06:47,000)
Adult confirmation of a 'faded memory' of Dr. King at the porch (Segments 49-50, 00:07:54,000)
Tears while speaking about how little has changed (Segment 144, 00:18:30,000)
relatable_experiences.connection_points: Audiences who have known a hometown's hostility, or who have watched relatives leave to find work, can connect to Duncan's emotional registers without needing prior knowledge of the St. Augustine campaign.
relatable_experiences.justification: Universal themes are present and accessible. Some of the most resonant moments — the mother's protective hand, the son's departure — bridge to general human experience well. The interview is more about civic memory work than about personal transformation, which slightly limits relatability.

emotional_resonance.overall_assessment: Duncan's emotional resonance is solid. The childhood-fear moments and the adult grief are the strongest threads; the transformation arc from witness to memory-keeper is real but understated. The dramatic content is present (Klan terror, school violence, public confrontations) but is mostly recounted rather than dramatized.

barriers: Some Whisper transcription artifacts (e.g., 'trimmer's in street' for what is likely Treasury Street, 'Zebra Lendonkin' for what is likely the son's first name, 'New Haven' for what is likely a different cross street) — these are audio-quality issues, not Duncan's articulation
Some place names and figure names tentative ('I think it was Spellman in Atlanta')
language_clarity.justification: Duncan speaks clearly and accessibly. There is no significant jargon and she self-contextualizes where needed. The transcription introduces some confusion on proper nouns but the testimony is internally clear.

high_energy_sections: Klan night march and 'don't look back' (Segments 37-44, 00:05:49,000 - 00:07:08,000)
J. T. Johnson's 'just say thank you' moment (Segments 134-139, 00:17:10,000 - 00:17:39,000)
Crying while speaking of how little has changed (Segment 144, 00:18:30,000)
slow_sections: Genealogical detail at the opening (Segments 19-28, 00:02:15,000 - 00:04:20,000) — substantively important but slower-paced
narrative_flow.attention_sustainability: Listeners can sustain attention across the 33-minute interview, especially through the childhood-memory and movement-commemoration arcs.
narrative_flow.justification: Mosnier's prompts open and pivot the narrative without overspeaking. Duncan maintains momentum across most sections. The opening genealogy is slower; the central childhood-memory arc and the 40th ACCORD account are the strongest pace-wise.

themes_connected: Generational continuity of Black presence in the U.S. South
Local memory work as resistance to institutional forgetting
Tourism economy and the commodification of civil rights history
Persistent economic exclusion of Black professionals in post-movement towns
Sanctuary geography (Fort Mose as an ancestor's possible motive)
broader_themes_connection.contemporary_relevance: Duncan directly connects the 1963-64 movement to 2011 St. Augustine, where her son cannot find work and Black businesses are nearly absent.
broader_themes_connection.meaning_making: Duncan explicitly frames the 40th ACCORD's work as both gratitude and a call to ongoing change ('Saint Augustine, Florida needs to change and the change is going to come'), helping audiences see the museum and markers as live political acts.
broader_themes_connection.justification: Strong connection to broader themes. Duncan never lets the interview become a closed period-piece narrative; she repeatedly bridges to the 2011 present and to what the next generation faces.

accessibility.overall_assessment: Accessibility is good. Duncan's speech is clear, dialect impact is minimal, and Mosnier paces the interview well. Some transcription artifacts on proper nouns are audio-quality issues, not speaker issues. The interview's contemporary-relevance bridge is strong.

engagement_scores.overall_assessment: Duncan's interview is a Very Good (76/100) entry in the Civil Rights History Project corpus. Its strongest dimension is narrative quality, with vivid sensory anchors in the Klan-march and doctor's-office scenes. Its historical value is solid but bounded: Duncan is a child witness and an organizational memory-keeper rather than a national-tier participant. The most distinctive contribution is her account of the post-2002 40th ACCORD commemorative effort — including the South Seas founding meeting, the Northrop Grumman marker funding, and the planned 50th-anniversary museum — which is genuinely understudied. Emotional resonance is grounded in honest tears and direct grief about St. Augustine's failures. Accessibility is good aside from transcription artifacts on a few proper nouns.
key_strengths: Vivid Lincolnville childhood scenes (Klan night march, doctor's office segregation, porch encounter with Dr. King) anchor the testimony in sensory memory rather than abstraction

Detailed institutional history of the 40th ACCORD — the South Seas restaurant meeting, the named co-founders (David Nolan, J. T. Johnson, Cora Tyson Johnson, Jane Billings, Dwight Hines, Sandra Parks), and the marker-funding by Northrop Grumman — fills a real gap in the published scholarship
Honest emotional register, including on-camera tears, connects the 1964 sacrifices to 2011 unfinished work and the migration of Black professionals (including her own son)
key_limitations: Duncan was a child during the 1963-64 campaign and a memory-keeper rather than a participant, so her firsthand access to the campaign itself is limited to local Lincolnville household-level memory

Several proper nouns are tentative or Whisper-garbled in the transcript (Jane Billings 'I think it was Spellman,' the street rendered 'trimmer's in,' the son's first name rendered 'Zebra Lendonkin,' the predecessor company rendered 'Fertile Hill'), reducing citation-level precision

claims.claim: Duncan is a fifth-generation native of Lincolnville in St. Augustine, Florida, born June 28, 1956.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I'll start off. My name is Gwendolyn and I was born and raised in Lincolnville of St. Augustine, Florida, June 28th, 1956. I am a fifth generation native (Segment 18, 00:02:02,000)
claims.rationale: Duncan states birth date, location, and fifth-generation status verbatim.

claims.claim: Her great-great grandfather Thomas A. Finley migrated from the Carolinas through Georgia and settled with thirteen children in Moultrie, just south of St. Augustine in St. Johns County.
claims.supporting_excerpt: my great-great grandfather, Thomas A. Finley, lived in St. Augustine and in Lincolnville... I was told that he moved from the Carolinas... my great-great grandfather had 13 children. Some were born along the way coming to St. Augustine in South Carolina, Georgia, and eventually he ended up in Moultrie, which is just south of St. Augustine, St. John's County. (Segments 19-22, 00:02:15,000 - 00:02:53,000)
claims.rationale: All named details — Thomas A. Finley, Carolinas, South Carolina/Georgia route, 13 children, Moultrie, St. Johns County — are directly transcribed.

claims.claim: Her great-grandmother Abby Finley was a founder of St. Cyprian's Episcopal Church in Lincolnville, which has stood over 150 years.
claims.supporting_excerpt: His daughter, which was my great-grandmother, Abby Finley. She married her husband, Abby Finley... She was a founder of St. Cyprian's and the Episcopal Church, which has been in Lincolnville for maybe over 150 years now. (Segments 23-26, 00:03:10,000 - 00:03:54,000)
claims.rationale: Speaker explicitly names Abby Finley and St. Cyprian's Episcopal Church and the 'maybe over 150 years' framing.

claims.claim: Duncan is one of eight girls (no boys) raised in Lincolnville.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I am one of eight girls. My mother had eight girls, no boys. (Segment 33, 00:05:07,000)
claims.rationale: Direct verbatim claim.

claims.claim: Her grandmother on her mother's side died in November 1963, the same month President Kennedy was shot.
claims.supporting_excerpt: She died before my grandmother, my mother's mother died. And she died in November of 1963. Same month that President Kennedy was shot. I can remember that happening. (Segment 36, 00:05:37,000)
claims.rationale: Direct verbatim claim, including the Kennedy assassination month-match.

claims.claim: Duncan was seven and a half years old when Dr. King came to St. Augustine and remembers Ku Klux Klan marches through Lincolnville.
claims.supporting_excerpt: But I know you want to talk about the civil rights movement. What I remember most from a child at seven and a half years old when Dr. King came to St. Augustine was the marches by the Ku Klux Klan. (Segment 37, 00:05:49,000)
claims.rationale: Verbatim age and Klan-march claim.

claims.claim: Duncan's mother held her and her sister's hands during a Klan march and warned 'don't look back.'
claims.supporting_excerpt: And I can remember, as we were walking home, my mom holding my sister's hands in mine, and she said, don't look back. I could just remember the terror of don't look back. (Segment 43, 00:06:47,000)
claims.rationale: Direct verbatim quote of 'don't look back' and the mother's grip.

claims.claim: Some of Duncan's classmates' homes were firebombed.
claims.supporting_excerpt: And some of my classmates, you know, at Excelsior Elementary School, some of their homes on the other street were bonned, fire bonned. (Segment 44, 00:06:55,000)
claims.rationale: Direct claim. Transcript renders 'bombed' as 'bonned' but meaning is clear.

claims.claim: Dr. King came across the street from Mr. Proctor's house to ask Duncan's mother about attending a rally.
claims.supporting_excerpt: And Dr. King must have, he was staying across the street to Mr. Proctor's house. And I don't know if he stayed there that night. All I know is he came across the street to ask my mom if she was going to participate in the rally or to marches that night. (Segment 47, 00:07:26,000)
claims.rationale: Duncan herself is uncertain about whether Mr. Proctor was Dr. King's host that night ('I don't know if he stayed there that night'). The claim that Dr. King came across the street to ask is supported; that Mr. Proctor was the host is speculation.

claims.claim: Duncan completed fifth grade at Excelsior Elementary School and was sent to a previously white school by forced integration in sixth grade.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Yet I remember being in the fifth grade and I completed my fifth grade year at Excelsior Elementary School Elementary School. And in the sixth grade, we had to go, it was forced integration. So our school closed down and we had to go to our street school. (Segments 72-73, 00:10:55,000)
claims.rationale: Direct verbatim — Excelsior, fifth grade, sixth-grade forced integration.

claims.claim: At the integrated junior high there was no cafeteria, so students walked back to Orange Street to eat lunch, where white students formed a line to block them.
claims.supporting_excerpt: And so even through our teen years going to the junior high school, the junior high school didn't even have a cafeteria. So you had to walk back to Orange Street just to eat. And the white kids were form a line, you know, to prevent you from going to eat your lunch. (Segments 84-86, 00:12:09,000)
claims.rationale: Direct verbatim claim.

claims.claim: Mrs. Catherine Twine died in 2002, and her funeral catalyzed the 40th ACCORD founding meeting at the South Seas restaurant.
claims.supporting_excerpt: And there was an opportunity after one of my friends, Mrs. Mrs. Catherine Twine died... But David Nolan, after her internment in 2002, he and JT Johnson from Atlanta. Jane Billings of Brown... They all met at a restaurant. South Seas over on the island. And they discussed the upcoming 40th anniversary of the civil rights movement of Sinagasni. (Segments 97-106, 00:13:31,000 - 00:14:25,000)
claims.rationale: Direct verbatim — Catherine Twine, 2002, South Seas restaurant, 40th anniversary.

claims.claim: The South Seas founding-meeting attendees included David Nolan, J. T. Johnson, Jane Billings, Mrs. Cora Tyson Johnson, Dwight Hines, and Sandra Parks.
claims.supporting_excerpt: But David Nolan, after her internment in 2002, he and JT Johnson from Atlanta, Georgia. Jane Billings of Brown, she was a professor at one of the schools. I think it was Spellman in Atlanta. David Nolan, as I said, Mrs. Mrs. Cora Tyson Johnson, Dwight. I can't remember his last name. Dwight Hines, I believe it was. Sandra Parks, they all met at a restaurant. South Seas over on the island. (Segments 100-105, 00:13:53,000 - 00:14:23,000)
claims.rationale: All six attendees named, with appropriate speaker hesitation on Jane Billings's institution and Dwight Hines's last name preserved.

claims.claim: Northrop Grumman funded thirty historic civil rights markers placed in and around St. Augustine, including on private citizens' yards.
claims.supporting_excerpt: But in the Northrop Grumman incorporation, thank God, they decided to give us a chance and they funded 30 historic civil rights markers in and around the city. And I thank God that some of the city officials allowed this to happen and some private citizens offered and gave us permission to put these markers, these historic markers in their yard (Segments 163-164, 00:21:14,000 - 00:21:32,000)
claims.rationale: Direct verbatim — Northrop Grumman, 30 markers, private citizens' yards.

claims.claim: Mrs. Reena Ayers will be 107 years old this year and gave lodging and food to demonstrators and to Northern attorneys defending them in court.
claims.supporting_excerpt: We have a lady who's a hundred, she'll be a hundred and seven years old this year who gave her name is Mrs. Mrs. Reena Ayers, who gave lodging and food to the many demonstrators who came from the North, to attorneys who came from the North to defend them in the courts. (Segment 166, 00:22:10,000)
claims.rationale: Direct verbatim — name, age, lodging, demonstrators and Northern attorneys.

claims.claim: Dr. Dorothy Cotton was the keynote speaker at the 40th ACCORD's most recent Civil Rights Act anniversary commemoration.
claims.supporting_excerpt: In this past July, we had Dr. Dorothy Cotton come. She was our keynote speaker. (Segment 239, 00:30:39,000)
claims.rationale: Direct verbatim claim. Dorothy Cotton matches the ground-truth corpus entry.

claims.claim: Taylor Branch was scheduled as a 40th ACCORD speaker for July 2012.
claims.supporting_excerpt: She comes July 2012. We have, uh, Taylor Branch coming. That's, that will be our signature event. (Segments 241-242, 00:30:53,000 - 00:31:02,000)
claims.rationale: Direct verbatim claim.

claims.claim: Sonja Plummer was a featured speaker; she is the granddaughter of civil rights activist Reverend Goldie Eubanks.
claims.supporting_excerpt: And I guess speaker was Sonja Plummer. She was a granddaughter of one of the Civil Rights activists here, Reverend Goldie Eubanks. (Segment 240, 00:30:48,000)
claims.rationale: Direct verbatim claim. 'Guess speaker' is transcribed 'guest speaker'; meaning preserved.

claims.claim: The 40th ACCORD's civil rights museum committee is chaired by Richard Burton of Jacksonville (a national NAACP board member) with J. T. Johnson as co-chair, and is aiming for 2014.
claims.supporting_excerpt: And we have the chairman, Richard Burton out of Jacksonville, who was a national NAACP board member. He's our chair, JT Johnson Co. Chair... we're looking by 2014 to have something up and running. (Segments 253-254, 00:33:00,000 - 00:33:15,000)
claims.rationale: Direct verbatim claim.

claims.claim: Ambassador Andrew Young previewed his civil rights film 'Crossing in St. Augustine' in St. Augustine.
claims.supporting_excerpt: But the city, I guess attitudes have changed somewhat. And, you know, with the Ambassador Andrew Young coming back here and previewing his movie, that, that, I mean, really thanks took off after that. (Segments 209-210, 00:27:22,000)
claims.rationale: Direct verbatim claim. The film title 'Crossing in St. Augustine' is named in segment 192.

claims.claim: Hank Thomas described St. Augustine as 'paternalistic.'
claims.supporting_excerpt: St. Augustine was a, is a quiet town, a peaceful town. But as, uh, Hank Thomas said, it is paternalistic. (Segment 228, 00:29:34,000)
claims.rationale: Direct verbatim attribution.

claims.claim: Cora Tyson Johnson described the unchanged conditions of St. Augustine as 'like a hidden vapor.'
claims.supporting_excerpt: Mrs. Mrs. Cora Tyson Johnson refers to it as like a hidden vapor, you know, over this town. (Segment 95, 00:13:14,000)
claims.rationale: Direct verbatim attribution.

claims.claim: Duncan's son has an industrial-engineering and systems-engineering bachelor's degree from the University of Florida and a separate bachelor's degree in building and construction, and could not find work in St. Augustine.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I have a son, Zebra Lendonkin, who graduated with the engineering degree from the University of Florida, industrial engineering degree and systems engineering, got a bachelor's degree. And he came back to this town seeking a job and could not find a job... So he went back to school and got his bachelor's degree and building and construction and still couldn't get a job in this city. (Segments 149-152, 00:19:22,000 - 00:19:51,000)
claims.rationale: Direct verbatim claim. The son's name is rendered 'Zebra Lendonkin' in the SRT which is likely a Whisper artifact, so the summary does not quote the name.

claims.claim: Duncan's son wrote publicly that 'the city that he loved will never be the city that he will come back to ever again.'
claims.supporting_excerpt: He wrote something that really touched my heart not too long ago and put it on online that the city that he loved will never be the city that he will come back to ever again. (Segment 153, 00:19:59,000)
claims.rationale: Direct verbatim quote.

claims.claim: Fort Mose was the first free Black settlement in the United States.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I learned later in life that the first free black settlement in the United States was Fort Mose, you know, and that may be know why my ancestors came so far and risked so much that come to this place of sanctuary because it was a sanctuary for blacks at one point. (Segment 143, 00:18:10,000)
claims.rationale: Duncan makes this claim directly in the transcript. It is historically accurate that Fort Mose (established 1738 near St. Augustine) is generally recognized as the first legally sanctioned free Black community in what is now the United States. The status is partially_supported because the summary's claim is rooted in Duncan's statement, which is itself a historical claim she frames as something she 'learned later in life.' The summary does not contradict the historical record.

claims.claim: The 40th ACCORD predecessor company (whose hostility Duncan contrasts with Northrop Grumman) is named.
claims.supporting_excerpt: the history shows that Fertile Hill, the company that was previously there, they were not very friendly to the civil rights demonstrators or blacks that were employed there. (Segment 218, 00:28:02,000)
claims.rationale: Duncan names the company as 'Fertile Hill' in the SRT, which is a Whisper artifact — the canonical name in St. Augustine industrial history is Fairchild. The summary uses 'Fairchild' as the canonical form, which is an inference from context, not a transcript-explicit name. This is rated partially_supported because the underlying claim (the predecessor company was hostile) is fully supported but the name resolution is an inference.

claims.claim: Duncan honors Dr. Robert B. Hayling, the local foot soldiers (including Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson) at marker dedications.
claims.supporting_excerpt: And I'm just glad to be a part of bringing recognition to people like Dr. Robert Hayling, the St. Augustine Foot Soldiers, Audrey Nell Hamilton, it was in Joanne Anderson, Elmer. (Segment 165, 00:21:57,000)
claims.rationale: Direct attribution. Audrey Nell Hamilton matches the ground-truth corpus entry for The St. Augustine Four (Audrey Nell Edwards Hamilton). 'Joanne Anderson, Elmer' is likely Whisper for 'JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer' — the corpus form. Robert B. Hayling matches the corpus entry.

publication_decision.rationale: The interview is a Very Good 76/100 engagement entry from a credible witness. Main-summary scoring lands at accuracy 86 and quality 88, both below the 90/90 Smithsonian-grade publication threshold. The citation audit identifies three partially-supported claims: (1) Dr. King's lodging with Mr. Proctor, which Duncan herself is uncertain about; (2) the predecessor company name 'Fairchild,' which is inferred from context rather than transcript-explicit (the SRT renders it 'Fertile Hill'); and (3) the Fort Mose 'first free Black settlement' framing, which Duncan claims directly but which is a layered historical claim worth flagging for reviewer attention. None of the claims are unsupported. The correct destination is human review, where the partials can be revised (Proctor softened, Fairchild either re-verified or replaced with the SRT spelling and a transcript-artifact note, Fort Mose reframed as Duncan's own framing) and the accuracy/quality scores can rise above 90 with those revisions.
